nativeRange.habitat: Rocky and coral reefs at depths of approximately 4–75 m; typically aggregates near the reef bottom in small schools. Range extends from the Gulf of California south to Banderas Bay (Cabo San Lucas area), with recent records pushing northward.

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented feeding primarily on zooplankton (planktivore tendency) and has also been observed consuming fish eggs. In captivity, generally accepts a varied diet consisting of meaty items such as frozen mysis shrimp and brine shrimp, herbivore flake foods, and frozen preparations. Vitamin-enriched foods are commonly recommended to help preserve coloration. Two to three small feedings per day tend to reduce competition stress in group-housed specimens.

summary: A hardy, subtly colorful schooling damselfish endemic to the Gulf of California. Juveniles display vivid blue with a yellow tail; adults darken to blue-purple. Generally considered much milder than typical damsels and commonly considered reef-safe.
careNotes:
Generally advised to keep in small groups of at least 3; like other chromis, solitary individuals can become subdued and groups tend to develop dominance hierarchies. Best suited to a reef system with ample rockwork and open mid-water swimming space that mimics its natural rocky reef habitat.

Juvenile color phase is notably distinct: young fish are bright blue with a yellow caudal fin; upon maturation the yellow fades and the body deepens to a blue-purple. This makes juveniles sometimes identifiable as different fish in the trade.

Despite a 'semi-aggressive' rating by some retailers, the species is widely described as atypically mild-mannered for a damselfish and is noted as much less aggressive than similar-looking damsels. The semi-aggressive label largely reflects typical within-genus conspecific hierarchy behavior rather than aggression toward unrelated tankmates.

The species has not been reported to be successfully bred in captivity; all available specimens are typically wild-caught. It is gonochoristic (fixed sex — not a sequential hermaphrodite). Mating in the wild is polygamous.

Temperature note: LiveAquaria recommends 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); FishBase wild data indicates 18.9–26.1°C; general chromis husbandry guides suggest up to 28°C. The averaged range of 21–27°C is used here.

Tank size note: LiveAquaria lists 50 gallons (189 L) minimum; Rock N Critters lists 100 L. Because this species is best kept in groups and benefits from open swimming space, a cautious 200 L is used as the minimum to accommodate a small shoal.
breedingNotes: Has been documented spawning in the wild with demersal, substrate-adhering eggs; males guard and aerate the eggs until hatching. The species appears to have never been successfully bred in home aquaria, and all available specimens are wild-caught. Captive breeding should be considered very difficult to not achieved in captivity.

fish.breedingNotes: Has not been successfully bred in captivity; all trade specimens are wild-caught. In the wild, males prepare nests and guard demersal eggs.
